truth.papers.A53.title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
truth.papers.A53.paper_scope: Investigates the isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity Fe-Mn-Al alloys at 600–1000°C, producing an oxide map at 800°C and determining the influence of Mn and Al on scale morphology and protective alumina formation.

prediction.papers.A53.title: High Temperature Oxidation of Iron-Manganese-Aluminum Based Alloys
prediction.papers.A53.paper_scope: Investigation of isothermal oxidation resistance of high purity Fe-Mn-Al and Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys at 600-1000°C in pure oxygen at 200 torr, focusing on scale morphologies, oxide map construction, and the effects of manganese, aluminum, and chromium on protective alumina scale formation.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe20mn6al.alloys_notes: Specimen oxidized for only 18 hr in Table I

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe30mn12al.alloys_notes: High purity iron-manganese-aluminum alloys containing 0 to 40 wt.% manganese and 0 to 15 wt.% aluminum. Approximately 50 compositions were investigated.
prediction.alloys.alloy_fe15mn5al5cr.alloys_notes: Fe-Mn-Al alloys with small additions of chromium (3 to 12 wt.%), prepared to study the effect of chromium on oxidation resistance.

truth.processes.proc_800C_24hr.description: Isothermal oxidation at 800°C in static pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 24 hr
truth.processes.proc_800C_100hr.description: Isothermal oxidation at 800°C in static pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 100 hr
truth.processes.proc_600C_100hr.description: Isothermal oxidation at 600°C in static pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 100 hr
truth.processes.proc_1000C_100hr.description: Isothermal oxidation at 1000°C in static pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 100 hr

prediction.processes.proc_femnal_cast.description: Levitation melting from high purity materials; disc shaped specimens in the as-cast condition approximately 7 mm in diameter and 1 mm in thickness.
prediction.processes.proc_femnal_cast.processes_not: Approximately 50 Fe-Mn-Al alloys and several additional chromium-containing alloys were prepared.
prediction.processes.proc_800C_24hr.description: Isothermal oxidation at 800°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 24 hr under static conditions.
prediction.processes.proc_800C_24hr.processes_notes: Kinetics recorded continuously using a Cahn R.G. electrobalance for all alloys oxidized for 24 hr. Weight gains before and after oxidation measured.
prediction.processes.proc_1000C_100hr.description: Isothermal oxidation of selected alloys for 100 hr at 600, 800, or 1000°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr.
prediction.processes.proc_1000C_100hr.processes_not: Two selected alloys (Fe-10Mn-6Al and Fe-20Mn-8Al) were oxidized to determine effects of temperature and time upon oxidation behavior.
prediction.processes.proc_femnalcr_ox.description: Isothermal oxidation of Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys at 800°C in pure oxygen at a pressure of 200 torr for 24 or 100 hr.
prediction.processes.proc_femnalcr_ox.processes_not: Preliminary tests showed small additions of chromium improved oxidation resistance; a series of experiments was performed to study its effect.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_femnal_cast.proc: Prepared from high purity materials; disc shaped specimens approximately 7 mm diameter and 1 mm thickness.
Pure oxygen at 200 torr under static conditions. Weight gains recorded continuously using Cahn R.G. electrobalance.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe10mn6al_600C_1: Pure oxygen at 200 torr. One of two selected alloys for temperature effect study.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe20mn8al_600C_1: Pure oxygen at 200 torr. Second selected alloy for temperature effect study.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe20mn8al_800C_1: Pure oxygen at 200 torr. Weight gain unreliable due to spalling.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe20mn8al_1000C_: Pure oxygen at 200 torr. Weight gain unreliable due to spalling.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe7_5mn5al5cr_10: Pure oxygen at 200 torr. All alloys showed signs of scale degradation after 100 hr.

truth.structures.sample_fe7_5mn5al_24hr.overal: Region I alloy: ferritic matrix with an alumina scale interspersed with large and small iron-rich oxide nodules. Scale shows breakaway and healing kinetics.
truth.structures.sample_fe7_5mn5al_24hr.micros: inner layer of large nodules, enriched in Mn and Al
grey semicontinuous outer layer or particles in large nodules; complete outer layer in small nodules
healing layers or particles at metal‑oxide interface
truth.structures.sample_fe15mn6al_24hr.overall: Region II duplex α+γ alloy; scale composed of alumina with bulky manganese-rich oxides and oxide threads. Breakaway occurs above austenite precipitates.
truth.structures.sample_fe15mn6al_24hr.microst: protective over ferrite, breached over austenite
linear arrays above austenite plates; contain Mn2O3, Mn3O4, (Fe,Mn)3O4, and Al‑rich phases
truth.structures.sample_fe10mn6al_24hr.overall: Region III fully ferritic alloy; forms a very adherent continuous protective alumina scale with only small oxide protrusions. Extremely low weight gain (0.054 mg cm⁻²).
truth.structures.sample_fe10mn6al_24hr.microst: featureless adherent protective alumina; few small oxide protrusions (< micron) not structurally related
truth.structures.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.overal: Region IV alloy (high Al, >9%): alumina scale penetrated by numerous small manganese‑rich oxide nodules. Smooth rounded voids beneath spalled alumina. Nodule growth involves Mn vapor transport.
truth.structures.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.number: α or α+β‑Mn matrix, alumina, Mn‑rich nodules
truth.structures.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.micros: continuous but penetrated by small nodules and prone to spallation
composed of Mn‑rich oxides (Mn2O3, Mn3O4) with Al‑rich (Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4) healing layer at nodule‑alloy interface; formed over voids
smooth rounded voids in substrate beneath spalled regions; voids filled/partially filled with manganese oxide
truth.structures.sample_fe20mn8al_24hr.overall: Region II duplex α+γ alloy: bulky manganese‑rich scales and wormlike oxide threads form above austenite. Scale structures depend on Mn concentration; here Fe2O3 disappears and Mn‑rich oxides dominate.
truth.structures.sample_fe20mn8al_24hr.microst: primary ferrite; can form surface ferrite layer depleted in austenite
stratified or massive; composed of Mn2O3, Mn3O4, (Fe,Mn)3O4, (Fe,Mn)Al2O4/Al2O3
truth.structures.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.over: Quaternary alloy duplex α+γ, oxidized at 800°C 24 hr: highly convoluted scale detached from substrate; substrate surface became fully ferritic and Mn‑depleted; scale base enriched in Al; thicker sections enriched in Mn.
truth.structures.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.numb: multiple oxide layers over ferritic substrate
truth.structures.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.micr: thick, detached, uneven thickness; composed of higher oxides of Fe, Mn, Cr with alumina or aluminate spinel at scale base
formed above austenite, then integrated into convoluted scale
truth.structures.sample_fe7_5mn3al_24hr.overal: Alloy with low Al (3%), region I: very large iron oxide nodule. Nodule consists of outer Fe2O3 and inner (Fe,Mn)3O4 with Al‑rich internal oxide particles.
truth.structures.sample_fe7_5mn3al_24hr.number: mainly iron oxides over ferritic substrate
truth.structures.sample_fe7_5mn3al_24hr.micros: outer white layer of large nodule
inner layer, enrichment of Mn and Al
internal oxide particles at metal‑oxide interface

prediction.structures.sample_femnal_cast.overall_st: As-cast alloy structures depend on composition according to the Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram at 800°C, ranging from fully ferritic to duplex ferrite-austenite or ferrite plus beta-manganese.
prediction.structures.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.overal: Region IV alloys with >9% Al forming alumina scales penetrated by small manganese-rich oxide nodules; voids present beneath spalled areas.
prediction.structures.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.micros: white outer layer of large nodules
present in some scale structures associated with incomplete healing layers
continuous healing layer at metal-oxide interface
bulky oxides on alloys with >20% Mn
small protrusions generally less than a micron, abundant at high Mn concentrations
thin dark aluminum rich oxide layer at nodule-alloy interface
Thick convoluted alumina scale formed at 1000°C after 100 hr; adheres to substrate except at edges; two distinct layers.
outer layer enriched in manganese and aluminum, probably Mn3O4-Al2O3 solid solution
Convoluted alumina scale at 1000°C after 100 hr; identical to Fe-10Mn-6Al but spalled badly on cooling; external layer enriched in Mn and Al, inner layer with very little Fe or Mn.
outer layer enriched in Mn and Al
prediction.structures.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.over: Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys show varied scale morphologies: thin protective alumina on optimized compositions; hollow convoluted oxide mounds or wrinkled scales on low-Al or high-Mn compositions; duplex alloys form scales above austenite.
prediction.structures.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.micr: thin protective alumina scale on Fe-10Mn-4Al-12Cr, Fe-5Mn-5Al-5Cr, and Fe-7.5Mn-5Al-5Cr
higher oxides of Fe, Mn, Cr
hollow convoluted or wrinkled oxide mounds on low Al or low Cr alloys
associated with spalled alumina and angular voids

prediction.interfaces.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.defect: Austenite promotes breakdown of preexisting alumina scales
Bulky manganese rich oxides grow directly above precipitated austenite plates; austenite is detrimental to oxidation resistance.
Void formation beneath alumina scales facilitates nodule nucleation
Smooth rounded voids beneath spalled alumina provide sites for manganese vapor reaction with oxygen at flaws, leading to small Mn oxide nodule growth.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.phase_: Scales evolve from initial alumina to complex multilayered structures depending on alloy composition and oxidation time. Breakaway and healing kinetics observed. Secondary ferrite forms beneath scales on duplex alloys due to manganese depletion from austenite. At 1000°C, scales become convoluted and duplex-layered.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.phas: higher oxides of Fe, Mn, Cr
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.defe: Spallation and void formation associated with small manganese rich nodules
Similar to ternary alloys; voids in substrate contain angular voids and internal oxides where spallation occurred.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.phas: Small chromium additions reduce the aluminum concentration required to form protective alumina scales. Duplex Fe-Mn-Al-Cr alloys form scales above austenite similar to ternary region II alloys.

truth.properties.sample_fe0mn7al_24hr.chemical: Region I or II: alumina with oxide nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe1mn6al_24hr.chemical: Region I: alumina with large iron oxide nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe2_5mn13al_24hr.chemi: Region IV: alumina with small Mn‑rich nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe5mn6al_24hr.chemical: Region I: alumina with large nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe7_5mn5al_24hr.chemic: Region I: alumina with large iron oxide nodules and small Mn‑rich nodules (transition)
truth.properties.sample_fe7_5mn6al_24hr.chemic: Region III or borderline Region I small nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe10mn9al_24hr.chemica: Region IV or III? (but high Mn may shift)
truth.properties.sample_fe15mn6al_24hr.chemica: Region II: alumina with bulky manganese‑rich scales
truth.properties.sample_fe15mn8al_24hr.chemica: Region III: protective alumina with many small oxide protrusions (weight gain relatively high due to protrusions)
truth.properties.sample_fe20mn6al_24hr.chemica: Region II: rapid oxidation, ferrite surface layer formation
truth.properties.sample_fe20mn8al_24hr.chemica: Region II: bulky Mn‑rich oxides and oxide threads
truth.properties.sample_fe25mn8al_24hr.chemica: Region II: spalled alumina and bulky oxides
truth.properties.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.chemic: Region IV: alumina scale with small Mn‑rich nodules, spallation
truth.properties.sample_fe10mn6al_600C_100hr.c: Alumina and randomly scattered bulky/threadlike oxides
truth.properties.sample_fe10mn6al_800C_100hr.c: Similar to 24 h scale; some lifting, buckling and localized small bulky oxides
Thick convoluted alumina scale, adherent except at edges
truth.properties.sample_fe20mn8al_600C_100hr.c: Bulky Mn‑rich oxides (similar to Region II)
truth.properties.sample_fe20mn8al_800C_100hr.c: 0.242 mg cm^{-2} in 100 hr (unreliable due to spalling)
Similar to 24 h; fewer bulky oxides, severe spallation
Convoluted alumina scale; external layer enriched in Mn and Al; spalled on cooling
truth.properties.sample_fe5mn4al5cr_24hr.chemi: Hollow oxide mounds; small Mn‑rich nodules
truth.properties.sample_fe5mn5al5cr_100hr.chem: Signs of scale degradation after 100 hr
truth.properties.sample_fe7_5mn4al5cr_100hr.ch: Hollow oxide mounds; Mn oxide threads and small Mn‑rich nodules after 100 hr
truth.properties.sample_fe7_5mn5al5cr_100hr.ch: Not listed in Table III (row empty)
After 100 hr all alloys showed signs of scale degradation
truth.properties.sample_fe10mn4al5cr_24hr.chem: Breakaway; scales identical to certain alloys from region II (duplex)
truth.properties.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.chem: Extremely convoluted scale, detached; high weight gain

prediction.properties.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.chemic: Few alloys obeyed simple rate equations. Log-log plots of weight gain vs time yielded a large range of n values from (ΔW/A)^n = kt, indicating kinetics approaching parabolic were observed only infrequently.

Four morphological types at 800°C: Region I (non-protective alumina + bulky iron or manganese rich nodules), Region II (non-protective alumina + bulky Mn-rich oxides and threads over duplex ferrite-austenite), Region III (protective continuous alumina on fully ferritic alloys), Region IV (alumina + small penetrating Mn oxide nodules on alloys with >9% Al).
prediction.properties.sample_fe10mn6al_800C_100hr.c: After 100 hr at 800°C, scales similar to 24 hr but showing signs of degradation including lifting, buckling, and growth of small bulky oxides at localized sites on the surface.
Thick convoluted alumina scale adhering to substrate except at edges after 100 hr; much thicker than at 600 or 800°C; composed of two distinct layers (inner alumina, outer Mn-Al enriched layer probably Mn3O4-Al2O3 solid solution).
Convoluted alumina scale at 1000°C identical to Fe-10Mn-6Al but spalled badly on cooling. External scale layer enriched in Mn and Al; inner layer contained very little Fe or Mn.
prediction.properties.sample_fe15mn5al5cr_24hr.chem: Fe-10Mn-4Al-12Cr, Fe-5Mn-5Al-5Cr, and Fe-7.5Mn-5Al-5Cr formed thin protective alumina scales after 24 hr. Alloys with less than 5% aluminum subject to breakaway unless alloyed with relatively high levels of chromium. After 100 hr all alloys showed signs of scale degradation.

prediction.performance.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.servi: Static oxidation with no applied mechanical stress
prediction.performance.sample_fe30mn12al_24hr.compa: Fe-Mn-Al alloys without chromium and binary Fe-Al alloys

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.technique: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Surface and cross‑sectional scale morphology and qualitative elemental analysis
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Revealed four distinct scale morphologies (Regions I‑IV); large iron oxide nodules and small Mn‑rich nodules; Mn enrichment at surface and in layers; Al‑rich healing layers at interface; spallation and void formation.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.technique: X‑ray imaging (Fe Kα, Mn Kα, Al Kα) and electron‑probe microanalysis
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: Mn enrichment at gas‑oxide interface and in nodules; Al‑rich layers at alloy‑oxide interface; disappearance of (Fe,Mn)3O4 associated with continuous aluminate layers.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.purpose: Examination of alloy phases and scale cross‑sections
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.key_findi: Confirmed alloy structures (ferrite, austenite, β‑Mn) and scale layering; revealed relationship between scale morphology and substrate austenite.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.technique: Continuous weight recording using Cahn R.G. electrobalance
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: Weight gains rarely parabolic; breakaway and healing kinetics observed; ΔW/24 values used for comparison.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fe30mn12: Thermogravimetric analysis using Cahn R.G. electrobalance
Continuous recording of oxidation kinetics for 24 hr exposures
Breakaway and healing kinetics observed for non-protective alloys. Few alloys obeyed simple parabolic rate law; large range in n values from log-log plots.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_femnal_c: Examination of alloy structures and mounted, polished cross-sections of scales
Confirmed presence of alloy phases (ferrite, austenite, beta-Mn) in specimens oxidized at 800°C and in quenched specimens. Revealed scale structures and alloy phase boundaries.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Oxide map (Fig. 3) at 800°C showing four composition regions based on scale morphology: Region I (low Al) – alumina with large/small oxide nodules; Region II (duplex α+γ) – alumina with bulky Mn‑rich scales and oxide threads; Region III (ferritic, optimum 5–10% Mn, 6–10% Al) – continuous protective alumina with small protrusions; Region IV (Al >9%) – alumina with small Mn‑rich nodules and spallation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Manganese addition: <2% Mn drastically increases oxidation rate; 5–10% Mn reduces rate by replacing large iron oxide nodules with smaller Mn‑rich nodules; >10% Mn promotes austenite formation and bulky manganese oxides that degrade oxidation resistance.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Austenite is detrimental: it promotes breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and growth of bulky Mn‑rich oxides. Totally ferritic alloys necessary for good oxidation resistance.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Small chromium additions (e.g., 12% Cr in Fe‑10Mn‑4Al‑12Cr) enable protective alumina scale formation at lower Al contents. Alloys with less than 5% Al require high Cr to avoid breakaway.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: At 600°C higher Al levels are needed to form protective scales; the α/(α+γ) phase boundary shifts, increasing austenite fraction in some alloys (e.g., Fe‑20Mn‑8Al). At 1000°C both test alloys were ferritic and formed convoluted alumina scales.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Nodule growth mechanism in Region IV involves void formation beneath alumina scale, Mn vapor transport through voids, reaction with oxygen to form Mn2O3, and subsequent filling of voids with Mn oxide followed by Al healing layer.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Healing layers of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 at the metal‑oxide interface can stop growth of large nodules and cause transition to protective behavior. Incomplete isolation of (Fe,Mn)3O4 layer leads to enhanced Fe2O3 formation.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Kinetics: Log-log plots of weight gain versus time (Fig. 1) show breakaway and healing behavior for non-protective alloys and near-parabolic behavior for protective alumina formers. The rate equation (ΔW/A)^n = kt yielded a large range of n values, indicating few alloys obeyed simple rate equations; ΔW/24 values were used for comparison of oxidation rates.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Table I (24 hr at 800°C): Weight gains of Fe-Mn-Al alloys span from approximately 0.021 mg cm^-2 (Fe-9Mn-4Al) to over 1 mg cm^-2 (Fe-20Mn-6Al, 18 hr). Minimum weight gains occur in the composition range Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al (e.g., Fe-5Mn-7Al: 0.033; Fe-10Mn-6Al: 0.054; Fe-5Mn-9Al: 0.021 mg cm^-2). Additions of less than 2% Mn greatly increased oxidation rate of binary Fe-Al alloys, whereas 5–10% Mn significantly reduced it.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Table II (100 hr): Fe-10Mn-6Al weight gains: 0.171 (600°C), 0.117 (800°C), 0.450 (1000°C) mg cm^-2. Fe-20Mn-8Al: 0.101 (600°C), 0.242 (800°C, unreliable due to spalling), 0.253 (1000°C, unreliable due to spalling) mg cm^-2.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Table III (Fe-Mn-Al-Cr at 800°C): Fe-10Mn-4Al-12Cr showed lowest 24h weight gain 0.061 mg cm^-2 among Cr alloys; Fe-5Mn-5Al-5Cr: 0.068; Fe-7.5Mn-5Al-5Cr: 0.063. Fe-15Mn-5Al-5Cr showed highest 24h gain 2.663 mg cm^-2. Fe-5Mn-4Al-5Cr data 0.292 mg cm^-2 corresponds to 15 hr oxidation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Oxide map (Fig. 3) at 800°C classifies alloys into four regions superimposed on the phase diagram: Region I (insufficient Al, alumina + bulky nodules), Region II (duplex α+γ, alumina + bulky Mn-rich oxides + threads), Region III (fully ferritic, continuous protective alumina, Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al), Region IV (>9% Al, alumina + small penetrating Mn oxide nodules).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Fig. 17 schematic of nodule structures at 800°C: Type 1 (large iron oxide nodules, <5% Mn) has Fe2O3 outer, (Fe,Mn)3O4 inner, discontinuous Al2O3/aluminate. Type 2 (5–7.5% Mn) similar with partial healing layer. Type 3 (≥7.5% Mn) small Mn-rich nodules with continuous Mn2O3 outer layer and complete healing layer of Al2O3/aluminate at base; absence of (Fe,Mn)3O4 due to continuous aluminate.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Fig. 18 schematic of duplex alloy oxidation stages: (1) alumina forms over ferrite matrix, (2) austenite nucleates and grows beneath alumina, (3) Mn2O3 threads grow at weaknesses in alumina, (4) threads incorporated into bulky Mn-rich oxide with Mn depletion and austenite conversion back to ferrite, (5) continuous healing layer of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 forms.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fig. 20 schematic of Region IV small nodule growth mechanism: (1) void formation beneath alumina scale, (2) flaw formation in overlying alumina, (3) outward growth of Mn2O3, (4) filling of void via short-circuit oxygen diffusion, (5) formation of Al2O3 or (Fe,Mn)Al2O4 particles at oxide-metal interface, (6) healing layer formation and conversion of nodule to Mn2O3.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Key conclusions: The most oxidation resistant alloys have compositions within the range Fe-(5–10)%Mn-(6–10)%Al, are totally ferritic, and develop continuous protective alumina scales. Austenite is detrimental as it promotes breakdown of preexisting alumina scales and growth of bulky manganese rich oxides. Small additions of chromium are beneficial and reduce the concentration of aluminum required to form protective alumina scales.
